items.good_looks_like: Multi-period commitment in months or years (ideally) with clear value over time
items.walk_away_point: Minimum viable term to deliver results eg 12 months
items.their_likely_position:
Seller (you): Wants longer-term commitment for revenue security
Buyer: Wants flexibility and optionality
Low Position
Seller: Accepts 3–6 month deal with no upside
Buyer: Commits short-term only, keeps exit easy
Medium Position
Seller: Secures 6–12 months with some review points
Buyer: Accepts mid-term but builds in break clauses
High Position
Seller: Locks in 12–24 months with clear value exchange (pricing, priority, support)
Buyer: Commits long-term in exchange for commercial benefits
items.key_trade_offs: Length vs price / added value / priority
items.when_ai_should_challenge: Short deal accepted too quickly with no upside e.g. higher unit price for a shorter term
items.example_response: You’ve reduced the term — what have you secured in return?

items.direction_of_value: Fewer = better (focus, cost control, customisattion, logistics impact, complications on doing business abroad)
items.weak_looks_like: Over-expansion upfront - all markets launched at the same time.
items.walk_away_point: Markets you can not realistically support. Focus only on the markets where you are operationally ready
items.their_likely_position:
Seller: Wants controlled rollout
Buyer: Wants scale quickly
Low Position
Seller: Agrees to multi-market rollout immediately without support structure
Buyer: Pushes for “global from day one”
Medium Position
Seller: Agrees phased rollout but loosely defined
Buyer: Accepts prioritisation but still pushes expansion
High Position
Seller: Controls rollout (e.g. 2–3 priority markets first, expansion conditional on success)
Buyer: Aligns expansion with performance milestones
items.key_trade_offs: Markets vs cost / timeline / quality of roll-out/ considered or rushed launch (the latter will be risky!)
items.when_ai_should_challenge: Scale increases in number of territories without adjustment in length of term otherwise all the risk sits with the seller.
items.example_response: If we expand markets, what are we adjusting to support that?

items.direction_of_value: Fewer = better (simplicity, margin) BUT more products could give you greter presence in a physical store or online.
items.good_looks_like: Focused, high-impact range with strong margin on the narrow range of products
items.weak_looks_like: Overloaded offering with thin margins and a low inventory/ order volume on each product. Can lead to spreading yourself too thinly.
items.opening_position: Core products only. Which are the ones that are established that you are confident that they will sell
items.walk_away_point: Minimum commitment on too many or too few products. Leads to spreading yourself too thinly.
items.their_likely_position:
Seller: Wants focus
Buyer: Wants range
Low Position
Seller: Includes full portfolio to win deal
Buyer: Asks for “everything” to maximise choice
Medium Position
Seller: Reduces slightly but still stretched
Buyer: Accepts some prioritisation
High Position
Seller: Focuses on best-performing SKUs/services only
Buyer: Aligns range with what will actually sell
items.key_trade_offs: Range vs execution / margin as well as shelf space, nuber of stores for distribution and markets
items.when_ai_should_challenge: More gets added without value shift
items.example_response: What’s the impact of adding those extra products? What can you ask for if extra products are being asked for e.g. If you want 5 products then we need extra shelf space in the top 50% of stores as a minimum.

items.good_looks_like: Clear, enforceable notice period e.g. 3 months or 6 months
items.walk_away_point: Minimum workable protection or no notice period with the ability for contract to be terminated with zero notice
items.their_likely_position:
Seller: Wants protection
Buyer: Wants flexibility
Low Position
Seller: Accepts 30 days or less
Buyer: Can exit quickly with minimal impact
Medium Position
Seller: Secures 60–90 days
Buyer: Accepts moderate commitment
High Position
Seller: Secures 3–6 months with clear terms
Buyer: Accepts longer runway for stability)
items.key_trade_offs: Notice period Vs flexibility. Consider up front payment if no notice period or do not allow for returns.
items.example_response: How are you protecting yourself if they exit quickly? Can you limit your exposure by asking for up front payment, a larger initial order intake or shorter payment terms?

items.direction_of_value: Shorter period for seller is better whereas longer for buyer is better
items.good_looks_like: Paid or conditional exclusivity. Seller should not concede to exclusivity without any assurances on volume/ revenue etc
items.opening_position: No exclusivity ofefred by seller but likely to be asked for by the buyer as an opening position.
items.walk_away_point: Buyer asks seller to pay for exclusivity or gives no voluem commitemtn without concessions to any other variables. Remember exclusivity limits distribution opportunities outside of this relationship so is a BIG give,
items.their_likely_position:
Seller: Wants flexibility
Buyer: Wants control
Low Position
Seller: Grants full exclusivity for free
Buyer: Locks out competition at no cost
Medium Position
Seller: Limits exclusivity by geography or category
Buyer: Accepts partial exclusivity
High Position
Seller: Charges premium or ties exclusivity to volume/commitment
Buyer: Pays or commits significantly for exclusivity
items.when_ai_should_challenge: You give it away without askign for anything in return. Exclusivity will eb a BIG ask
items.example_response: What are you getting in return for exclusivity?

items.good_looks_like: Contracted or guaranteed volume with few order intakes and minimal bumber of delivery locations
items.weak_looks_like: A forecast with no commitment. Or a forecast and request to "call off" quantities with short delivery lead times.
items.opening_position: Guaranteed volume commitemtn within a specific time period e.g. 3 months (not open ended)
items.walk_away_point: Forecast only with no contracted order quantity or delivery schedule.
items.their_likely_position:
Seller: Wants guaranteed volume
Buyer: Wants optionality
Low Position
Seller: Gives pricing based on forecast only
Buyer: Provides non-binding estimates
Medium Position
Seller: Secures soft commitments or ranges
Buyer: Gives indicative volumes
High Position
Seller: Secures contractual minimum volumes
Buyer: Commits in exchange for better pricing
items.key_trade_offs: Volume and price as well as volume rebates
items.when_ai_should_challenge: Discounts without commitment and low pricing without firm commitment
items.example_response: Is that volume guaranteed or just indicative? Is there a minimum contractual commitment that you can hold them to?

items.direction_of_value: Lower = better (for you the seller)  as you don’t need to pay a lump sum based on business performance
items.good_looks_like: Rebates tied to growth or volume
items.weak_looks_like: Rebate not tied to any business metrics. Basically a "tax" on doing business.
items.opening_position: Conditional only on volume of business or time period of engageemnt
items.walk_away_point: Rebate demanded with no commitment so it appears as a tax
items.their_likely_position:
Seller: Wants to limit
Buyer: Wants certainty
Low Position
Seller: Agrees fixed rebates regardless of performance
Buyer: Secures guaranteed return
Medium Position
Seller: Links some rebate to thresholds
Buyer: Accepts partial conditions
High Position
Seller: Fully performance-based rebates (volume, growth, targets)
Buyer: Earns rebates through delivery
items.when_ai_should_challenge: Guaranteed rebates with no commitment from the buyer. Beware of phrases along the lines of "this is part of our standard terms of business"
items.example_response: What conditions is this rebate tied to? Are you sure that you should offer a rebate at all?

items.direction_of_value: Lower = better (for seller — controls cost, complexity, and speed)
items.good_looks_like: Standardised core assets with selective, high-impact localisation only where it genuinely drives results
items.weak_looks_like: Full customisation across every market without clear commercial return
items.opening_position: Seller: Core language + selective localisation only
items.walk_away_point: Any level of customisation that materially slows delivery or erodes margin
items.their_likely_position:
Low
Seller: Agrees to fully customise language for every market, often reactively (“we can tailor that”) without understanding cost or timeline impact
Buyer: Pushes for full localisation everywhere (all languages, all materials), regardless of actual need or ROI
Mid
Seller: Pushes back slightly — agrees to customise key elements (e.g. core messaging, top markets) but still overextends in some areas
Buyer: Accepts partial localisation but continues to push for additional tailoring over time
High
Seller: Clearly defines what is standard vs what is localised (e.g. “core assets stay consistent, we adapt X and Y for priority markets only”) and ties any additional customisation to cost or trade-offs
Buyer: Focuses localisation where it actually impacts performance (key markets, key touchpoints) and accepts efficiency elsewhere
items.when_ai_should_challenge: When you find yourself agreeing to “just tweak this for each market” without resetting expectations or commercial terms
items.example_response: We can absolutely tailor this — we just need to be clear which markets really need localisation versus where we keep things standard to stay efficient. Try not to overstretch yourself.

items.direction_of_value: Lower = better (for seller — controls resource, risk, and scope creep)
items.good_looks_like: Clearly defined integration scope with agreed roles and responsibilities with timed milestones on both sides
items.weak_looks_like: Open-ended commitment like “we’ll integrate with whatever you need”
items.opening_position: Defined integration scope (e.g. specific systems, limited support window, clear responsibilities)
items.walk_away_point: Any integration commitment that is undefined or open-ended
items.their_likely_position:
Low
Seller: Commits to full integration support without defining scope, timelines, or technical boundaries — ends up absorbing unexpected complexity
Buyer: Assumes seller will handle everything (systems, troubleshooting, ongoing fixes) with minimal internal effort
Mid
Seller: Defines some boundaries (e.g. initial setup only, limited systems) but still leaves grey areas that can expand later
Buyer: Accepts shared responsibility but continues to push for additional support as needs evolve
High
Seller: Clearly scopes integration (what’s included, what’s not, what requires additional cost), defines roles (who does what), and ties any extra support to commercial trade-offs
Buyer: Aligns on responsibilities, provides internal resource where needed, and understands additional support comes at a cost
items.key_trade_offs: Integration support vs price / timeline / internal resource - integration has a cost. Buy time to properly assess asks.
items.when_ai_should_challenge: When integration is discussed vaguely or assumed to be “covered” without detail
items.example_response: Happy to support integration — let’s just be clear on what sits with us versus the other team so we don’t create gaps later. Also take time to properly assess the integration needs with internal teams so you don't overpromise or undervalue.

items.direction_of_value: Lower = better (for sales person — limits financial and operational risk)
items.good_looks_like: Clearly defined, conditional returns policy with limits on timing, volume, and condition of goods
items.weak_looks_like: Open-ended or overly flexible returns that shift risk heavily onto you
items.opening_position: Controlled, conditional returns only (clear limits on time, volume, and condition)
items.walk_away_point: Any policy that exposes you to significant or unpredictable return risk
items.their_likely_position:
Low
Seller: Agrees to broad or open-ended returns (e.g. unsold stock can be returned at any time), often to secure the deal
Buyer: Minimises their risk completely — can over-order knowing they can return excess
Mid
Seller: Introduces some controls (e.g. time limits, partial returns, condition requirements) but still carries meaningful exposure
Buyer: Accepts some shared risk but retains flexibility in certain scenarios
High
Seller: Sets tight, clearly defined conditions (e.g. limited return window, capped volumes, strict product condition, approval required) and ensures risk is shared or sits appropriately with the buyer
Buyer: Accepts responsibility for forecasting and sell-through, with returns used only in agreed, exceptional circumstances
items.key_trade_offs: Returns flexibility vs price / volume commitment / exclusivity
items.when_ai_should_challenge: When returns are used as a “safety net” instead of committing to realistic volumes or proper sell-through. Returns shuld be defined with tight criteria for acceptance and subject to internal validation.
items.example_response: I understand the need for flexibility — we just need to make sure the returns structure doesn’t shift all the risk onto us.

items.direction_of_value: Lower = better (for seller — protects margin and avoids waste)
items.good_looks_like: Targeted, purposeful sampling tied to clear commercial outcomes (e.g. trial, launch support, conversion)
items.weak_looks_like: Large volumes of free samples given away without a clear plan or return
items.opening_position: Limited, targeted sampling tied to specific activity e.g. nuber of stores, demonstrators
items.walk_away_point: Any level of sampling that significantly impacts margin without clear return
items.their_likely_position:
Low
Seller: Agrees to large-scale free sampling (e.g. “we’ll supply whatever you need”) without linking it to performance or outcomes
Buyer: Requests high volumes of free product to support launches, promotions, or simply reduce their own cost/risk
Mid
Seller: Provides controlled sampling (e.g. capped volumes, specific campaigns) but doesn’t always tie it tightly to measurable outcomes
Buyer: Accepts limits but continues to push for additional samples over time
High
Seller: Links all sampling to clear objectives (e.g. specific campaigns, store trials, conversion targets), caps volumes, and treats additional requests as a trade-off or cost
Buyer: Uses samples strategically (e.g. targeted activations) and understands that increased sampling requires commitment elsewhere
items.key_trade_offs: Free samples vs volume commitment / visibility / marketing support
items.when_ai_should_challenge: When sampling is being requested as a default (“we’ll need samples”) rather than for a defined purpose
items.example_response: Happy to support with samples — let’s just be clear what they’re driving and how we measure whether they’ve worked.

items.direction_of_value: Lower = better (for seller — avoids cost without clear return)
items.good_looks_like: Competitions that are clearly tied to commercial outcomes (e.g. driving sales team behaviour, incentivising stores, increasing orders)
items.weak_looks_like: Running competitions “because it’s a good idea” without a clear link to sales or performance
items.opening_position: Competitions only where tied to clear commercial objectives
items.walk_away_point: Any competition that has no measurable impact or clear purpose
items.their_likely_position:
Low
Seller: Agrees to fund or support competitions with no clear objective, structure, or measurable return
Buyer: Uses competitions as general engagement tools (e.g. staff incentives) without linking to tangible commercial outcomes
Mid
Seller: Supports competitions with some structure (e.g. linked to sales targets or short-term pushes) but lacks clear measurement or follow-up
Buyer: Aligns competitions loosely to performance but still sees them as “nice to have” activity
High
Seller: Only supports competitions that are tightly linked to specific commercial outcomes (e.g. increase in orders, store activation, sell-through), with clear success metrics and defined timelines
Buyer: Uses competitions strategically to drive specific behaviours (e.g. upselling, prioritisation, distribution gains) and aligns with measurable goals
items.key_trade_offs: Competition support vs volume / visibility / trade marketing investment
items.when_ai_should_challenge: When competitions are proposed without a clear link to sales, distribution, or measurable behaviour change
items.example_response: Happy to support a competition — what specific behaviour or result are we trying to drive with it?

items.role: Shared (Buyer requests / Seller funds or contributes)
items.direction_of_value: Controlled = better (for seller — ensures spend drives real return)
items.good_looks_like: Targeted trade marketing investment directly linked to sell-through, distribution gains, or specific commercial outcomes
items.weak_looks_like: General “support” or budget commitments with no clear link to performance
items.opening_position: Targeted, activity-based investment linked to clear outcomes
items.walk_away_point: Any spend that isn’t tied to measurable commercial impact
items.their_likely_position:
Low
Seller: Commits trade marketing budget broadly (e.g. % of revenue or fixed pot) without clear control over how it’s used or what it delivers
Buyer: Secures funding for in-store or channel activity without strong accountability for results
Mid
Seller: Aligns spend to certain activities (e.g. promotions, launches) but measurement and ROI tracking are inconsistent
Buyer: Works with seller on planned activity but still expects flexibility in how funds are used
High
Seller: Ties all trade marketing investment to clear commercial outcomes (e.g. sell-through targets, distribution gains, specific campaigns), with agreed measurement and approval on how funds are deployed
Buyer: Uses trade marketing strategically to drive performance and is accountable for delivering agreed outcomes
items.key_trade_offs: Trade marketing spend vs volume / distribution / visibility
items.when_ai_should_challenge: When trade marketing is positioned as a standard requirement rather than something that needs to deliver specific results
items.example_response: Happy to invest in trade marketing — let’s just make sure we’re clear on what it’s driving and how we measure success.

items.direction_of_value: Lower = better (for seller — protects budget, brand control, and focus)
items.good_looks_like: Selective, strategic use of co-branded or additional marketing tied to clear commercial benefit
items.weak_looks_like: Agreeing to ongoing or undefined extra marketing activity just to strengthen the relationship
items.opening_position: No default inclusion — extra marketing is campaign-specific and agreed case-by-case
items.walk_away_point: Any ongoing or undefined commitment to promote the buyer’s brand without clear return
items.their_likely_position:
Low
Seller: Agrees to include buyer’s brand/logo in campaigns, ads, or content as standard — often without limits or clear benefit
Buyer: Secures ongoing visibility in marketing activity at little or no cost, regardless of performance
Mid
Seller: Allows co-branding or extra activity in specific campaigns (e.g. launches, promotions) but doesn’t always define limits clearly
Buyer: Accepts some structure but continues to push for inclusion in additional activity over time
High
Seller: Treats all extra marketing activity as scoped and conditional (e.g. specific campaigns, defined duration, clear objective), and ties inclusion to trade-offs (investment, volume, exposure)
Buyer: Uses co-branded activity selectively where it adds value and understands additional exposure requires contribution or commitment
items.key_trade_offs: Extra marketing vs funding / volume / visibility / strategic value
items.when_ai_should_challenge: When “can you include us in your marketing?” comes up without a clear objective or commercial return
items.example_response: Happy to include you in the right campaigns — let’s just make sure it’s tied to something that drives value for both of us.

items.role: Buyer requests / Seller funds or supports
items.direction_of_value: Lower = better (for salesperson — protects margin and avoids unnecessary price erosion)
items.good_looks_like:
Price promotions that are:
Clearly tied to specific commercial objectives (e.g. driving trial, clearing stock, hitting volume targets)
Time-bound and controlled (not always-on discounting)
Measured for effectiveness (uplift vs baseline, not just activity)
items.weak_looks_like:
Frequent or continuous discounting to drive volume
Promotions used as a substitute for poor demand or positioning
No clear measurement of whether the promotion actually worked. It feels like giving margin away to stay competitive
items.opening_position: Promotions only used selectively and tied to specific commercial objectives
items.walk_away_point: Any expectation of continuous or deep discounting that erodes margin without clear return
items.their_likely_position:
Low
Seller:
Agrees to regular or deep promotions to secure or maintain the deal, often without clear objectives or limits
Buyer:
Pushes for frequent price promotions as standard (e.g. “we’ll need to be on promo regularly”) to drive footfall or stay competitive
Mid
Seller:
Supports planned promotional windows (e.g. launches, seasonal events) but doesn’t always tightly control depth, frequency, or measurement
Buyer:
Aligns promotions to key trading moments but still expects a steady level of promotional support
High
Seller:
Only supports promotions that are:
Clearly justified (trial, activation, stock movement)
Time-bound
Evaluated based on performance
…and ensures promo depth/frequency are controlled
Buyer:
Uses promotions strategically (e.g. key events, new listings) and aligns them to driving specific outcomes rather than defaulting to discounting
items.key_trade_offs: Promotional support vs volume commitments / distribution gains / visibility / feature space
items.when_ai_should_challenge: When promotions are positioned as “standard” rather than a deliberate tool — especially if there’s no clear objective or measurement
items.example_response: Happy to support promotions — let’s just be clear what they’re driving and how we’ll measure whether they’ve actually worked.

items.direction_of_value: Lower = better (for seller — controls workload, cost, and scope creep)
items.good_looks_like:
Data and reporting that is:
Clearly defined upfront (what is shared, how often, in what format)
Focused on actionable insights, not just raw data
Aligned to commercial objectives (e.g. sales performance, distribution, campaign effectiveness)
It feels like useful insight that drives decisions, not just reporting for the sake of it
items.weak_looks_like:
Open-ended or ad hoc reporting requests
Large volumes of data shared without clear purpose
Reporting that becomes time-consuming but low impact
It feels like constant work with little commercial return
items.opening_position: Defined reporting structure (e.g. agreed metrics, cadence, and format)
items.walk_away_point: Open-ended or undefined reporting requirements that create ongoing, unbounded workload
items.their_likely_position:
Low
Seller:
Agrees to provide extensive or ongoing reporting without defining scope, frequency, or purpose
Buyer:
Requests broad access to data and frequent reporting, often without clarity on how it will be used
Mid
Seller:
Provides structured reporting (e.g. monthly reports, dashboards) but may still accommodate additional ad hoc requests
Buyer:
Accepts standard reporting cadence but continues to request additional insights or data over time
High
Seller:
Defines clear reporting scope:
What data is shared
How often
In what format
…and limits additional requests or ties them to commercial trade-offs
Buyer:
Focuses on key insights that drive performance and aligns reporting to specific business questions or objectives
items.key_trade_offs: Data/reporting vs price / service level / resource allocation / integration support
items.when_ai_should_challenge: When reporting requests expand beyond agreed scope or are not clearly linked to commercial decisions or outcomes
items.example_response: Happy to provide reporting — what decisions are we expecting this to inform?

items.role: Buyer controls / Seller pushes for commitment
items.direction_of_value: Higher = better (for seller — drives scale, revenue, and brand presence)
items.good_looks_like:
Distribution that is:
Clearly committed and defined (e.g. number of stores, regions, channels)
Realistic and deliverable (not just indicative)
Phased where appropriate, but with clear expansion triggers
It feels like real scale you can plan against, not just potential
items.weak_looks_like:
Vague or non-committal distribution (e.g. “we’ll aim to roll out”)
Over-promised distribution that doesn’t materialise
Distribution agreed without alignment to support (marketing, stock, etc.)
It feels like hope, not a plan
items.opening_position: Defined distribution plan with clear store numbers, locations, or channels
items.walk_away_point: No meaningful commitment on distribution or purely indicative rollout
items.their_likely_position:
Low
Seller:
Accepts vague or indicative distribution with no clear commitments, often just to secure the listing
Buyer:
Keeps distribution flexible (e.g. “we’ll see how it performs”), with no obligation to scale
Mid
Seller:
Secures partial or phased distribution (e.g. initial store list or region) but without firm expansion commitments
Buyer:
Commits to an initial rollout but keeps future expansion dependent on performance
High
Seller:
Secures clearly defined distribution commitments (e.g. number of stores, channels, timelines), often linked to agreed support and performance
Buyer:
Commits to meaningful scale and aligns distribution with commercial plans (marketing, stock, promotions)
items.key_trade_offs: Distribution vs price / promotional support / trade marketing / exclusivity
items.when_ai_should_challenge: When strong commercial concessions are being made (e.g. price, support) but distribution remains vague or non-committal
items.example_response: If we’re investing at that level, what level of distribution can we realistically plan for?

items.direction_of_value: Higher = better (for seller — drives visibility, conversion, and brand perception)
items.good_looks_like:
Premium positioning that:
Places the product in high-visibility, high-traffic areas (e.g. eye-level shelf, front-of-store, homepage, category top)
Aligns with the brand’s intended positioning (e.g. not buried in discount sections if premium)
Is consistent and sustained, not just temporary
 It feels like the product is being prioritised and set up to succeed
items.weak_looks_like:
Poor or low-visibility placement (e.g. bottom shelf, deep category pages)
Inconsistent or short-term positioning
Placement that undermines brand perception (e.g. premium product in discount-heavy environments)
 It feels like the product is present but not really supported
items.opening_position: Premium positioning aligned to brand and product value (e.g. eye-level shelf, high-visibility online placement)
items.walk_away_point: Placement that materially limits visibility or undermines the product’s ability to sell
items.their_likely_position:
Low
Seller:
Accepts poor placement (e.g. limited shelf space, low-traffic online positions) in order to secure distribution
Buyer:
Allocates minimal or non-priority space/placement, often alongside lower-performing or lower-priced products
Mid
Seller:
Secures reasonable positioning (e.g. standard shelf placement or mid-tier online visibility) but not consistently across locations or channels
Buyer:
Provides fair but not premium positioning, balancing across multiple brands and priorities
High
Seller:
Secures premium placement (e.g. eye-level, feature displays, homepage/category highlights) and ensures it aligns with brand positioning
Buyer:
Actively supports the product with strong positioning, recognising its value in driving sales or category performance
items.key_trade_offs: Premium positioning vs promotional support / trade marketing / volume commitments / exclusivity
items.when_ai_should_challenge: When strong commercial terms are agreed (e.g. price, support) but positioning does not reflect that level of investment or value
items.example_response: If we’re investing at that level, how do we make sure the product is actually visible enough to deliver the return?
